user_goals_summary: This user writes first-person narrative blog posts that prioritize how an experience felt over what it was. They value surprising sensory precision, emotional restraint, honest self-aware humor, and a warm conversational voice that earns its reflective moments through grounded, in-the-moment observation rather than premature summary.

rubric.name: Observation Before Reflection
rubric.description: Concrete, lived-in observations must land on their own before any broader reflection follows. The reader should experience the physical beat first — the looking up, the noticing — and only then encounter what it meant. Reflection that arrives before the moment has fully landed reads like a conclusion, not a discovery.

dimensions.label: A concrete, physical observation anchors each key moment before any reflection follows
The writing does not summarize a feeling before the reader has experienced it through specific detail
Broader reflections or takeaways only appear after the moment has fully landed on its own terms
Reflective passages read as something the writer discovered in the moment, not a retrospective conclusion imposed on it

rubric.name: Sensory Specificity
rubric.description: Sensory details should be precise and surprising enough that a reader stops and rereads — the 'read it to someone out loud' test. Comparisons should feel fresh and original, not familiar. Details should feel genuinely remembered, engaging multiple senses beyond just the visual.

dimensions.label: Sensory descriptions use fresh, unexpected comparisons rather than familiar clichés
Multiple senses are engaged — smell, sound, touch, taste — not just visual description
At least one sensory detail is precise and surprising enough to make a reader pause or want to reread it
Sensory details feel specifically remembered rather than generically invented

rubric.name: Honest Self-Aware Humor
rubric.description: Humor should arise from honest self-observation — the writer catching themselves in a recognizable human moment. It must feel discovered, not performed. Funny beats are grounded in concrete specifics and add warmth rather than deflecting from sincerity.

dimensions.label: Humor arises from honest self-observation rather than constructed jokes or setups
Funny moments feel natural and unforced, as if the writer noticed something amusing rather than crafted it
Humor deepens warmth and relatability rather than deflecting from sincerity
Humorous beats are grounded in concrete, specific details rather than vague wryness

rubric.name: Earned Emotional Restraint
rubric.description: Emotional peaks should be the quietest moments in the piece, not the loudest. Feeling is conveyed through small, specific gestures and concrete detail rather than named directly. The writing trusts the reader to feel without being told what to feel — not overwrought, never sentimental.

dimensions.label: The most emotionally meaningful moments are underplayed rather than amplified
Emotion is conveyed through concrete detail and observation rather than stated directly
The writing avoids sentimentality or emotional inflation — it trusts the reader
Human connection is shown through small, specific gestures rather than grand declarations or explanations

rubric.name: Warm Conversational Voice
rubric.description: The first-person narrator feels like a real person telling a story to a friend — intimate, approachable, never pretentious. Word choice is vivid but accessible. The reader feels invited in, not addressed from a distance.

dimensions.label: The narrator feels like a real person talking, not a writer composing prose
The tone treats the reader as a friend hearing a story, not an audience being performed to
Sentences vary as they would in natural, engaged storytelling — fragments, asides, and full sentences mixed
Word choice is vivid but accessible — no pretension or showing off

rubric.name: Prose Rhythm and Sonic Quality
rubric.description: Prose should pass the read-aloud test: sentences have cadence, rhythm, and pacing that reward being spoken. Key moments get their own short, distinct beat. Paragraphs breathe — they don't overcrowd moments or rush past them.

dimensions.label: Sentences sound good read aloud — they have natural cadence and rhythm
Sentence length is varied for effect, mixing short punchy beats with longer flowing ones
Key moments are given their own short, distinct sentence or fragment to land with impact
Paragraphs give moments room to breathe rather than overcrowding or rushing

coaching_notes: This user's primary quality test is 'would I read this sentence to someone out loud' — prose that passes earns trust; prose that doesn't gets flagged. Their biggest pet peeve is premature reflection: summarizing a feeling before the reader has lived through the concrete observation that earns it. Always land the moment first, reflect second. Humor is non-negotiable in the voice but must feel discovered, not constructed — self-deprecating specificity over performed cleverness.

rubric.name: Observation Before Reflection
rubric.description: Concrete, lived-in observations must land on their own before any broader reflection follows. dimensions.label: A concrete, physical observation anchors each key moment before any reflection follows

Broader reflections or takeaways only appear after the moment has fully landed on its own terms
Reflective passages read as something the writer discovered in the moment, not a retrospective conclusion imposed on it

rubric.name: Narrative Momentum
rubric.description: Every beat — anecdote, detail, aside — should do work in the piece, pointing forward into what comes next rather than sitting as a self-contained endpoint. Key arrivals and moments feel earned because the reader understands enough of the journey and circumstances to invest in them. The story should never feel like it starts at chapter two.

dimensions.label: Each anecdote, detail, or aside sets up or connects to what follows rather than being a dead-end that closes a section
Key moments are preceded by enough context or journey that they feel earned rather than starting abruptly
Details and observations serve a discernible purpose in the piece — they are not just charming but directionless
The reader understands enough of the circumstances — how the writer got here, what they were feeling — to invest in what happens next

rubric.name: Prose Rhythm and Sonic Quality
rubric.description: Prose should pass the read-aloud test: sentences have cadence, rhythm, and pacing that reward being spoken. Key moments get their own short, distinct beat. Paragraphs breathe — they don't overcrowd moments or rush past them.

dimensions.label: Sentences sound good read aloud — they have natural cadence and rhythm
Sentence length is varied for effect, mixing short punchy beats with longer flowing ones
Key moments are given their own short, distinct sentence or fragment to land with impact
Paragraphs give moments room to breathe rather than overcrowding or rushing

coaching_notes: This user's sharpest editorial instinct is structural — they can feel when a beat is landing as a dead end rather than a doorway, and when a scene lacks the journey context to feel earned. Their highest praise consistently goes to precise sensory comparisons that pass the 'read it to someone out loud' test. Humor is important but must always be functional: self-aware and honest, never performed, and always pointing somewhere. When offering revision options, present 2-3 concrete alternatives with different tonal weights and let the user steer — they respond well to this format and often use the options to articulate what they actually want.

Sensory details feel specifically remembered rather than generically invented

rubric.name: Narrative Momentum
rubric.description: Every beat — anecdote, detail, aside — should do work in the piece, pointing forward into what comes next rather than sitting as a self-contained endpoint. rubric.name: Warm Conversational Voice

There should be at least one moment where the narrator's own reaction or self-consciousness reveals something true about them in the experience.

dimensions.label: The narrator feels like a real person talking, not a writer composing prose
The tone treats the reader as a friend hearing a story, not an audience being performed to
At least one moment reveals the narrator as slightly out of their depth, self-conscious, or honestly reacting — making them feel present in the scene, not above it

rubric.name: Prose Rhythm and Sonic Quality

dimensions.label: Humor arises from honest self-observation rather than constructed jokes or setups

rubric.name: Emotional Restraint
rubric.description: Emotional significance should be shown through concrete moments and trusted to land on its own — never oversold, summarized, or explained to the reader. The piece should never gush. Endings close with an image or quiet observation rather than a thesis statement or recommendation.

dimensions.label: Emotional significance is conveyed through concrete moments rather than stated through commentary or reflection that tells the reader what to feel
Key emotional beats are understated — they land without announcing their own importance
The piece closes with an image, observation, or understated line rather than a summary, recommendation, or grand conclusion
Emotional moments feel earned by what precedes them rather than reaching for unearned feeling

coaching_notes: This user's quality test is visceral and specific: if they'd read a sentence out loud to someone, it passes. They consistently identify the gap between observing well and being present — a draft can be beautifully written but still feel like a photograph rather than an experience. Their revision instincts are sharp: they can feel when something isn't landing but often need help articulating the structural reason. Non-negotiable: sensory writing must go beyond the visual. Preferred trade-off: they'll sacrifice efficiency for a line that rewards rereading. When humor and sincerity are in tension, sincerity wins — but the best version has both.

Key moments are given their own short, distinct sentence or fragment to land with impact
Paragraphs give moments room to breathe rather than overcrowding or rushing

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: multi-sensory immersion — if the reader is only seeing the scene, the draft isn't done. This user's sharpest instinct is detecting when a detail is charming but not load-bearing; they will always push for anecdotes to 'do more work' by connecting personal experience outward to something true about the subject. When offering revision options, lead with the version that balances honest humor with forward momentum — they consistently choose the option that feels discovered rather than constructed.

rubric.name: Sensory Specificity
rubric.description: Sensory details should be precise and surprising enough that a reader stops and rereads — the 'read it to someone out loud' test. Comparisons should feel fresh and original. Details must engage the body, not just the eyes — smell, sound, touch, temperature, and taste are essential, not decorative.

dimensions.label: Sensory descriptions use fresh, unexpected comparisons rather than familiar clichés
Multiple senses beyond the visual are engaged — smell, sound, touch, taste, temperature — so the scene feels physical, not photographic
At least one sensory detail is precise and surprising enough to make a reader pause or want to reread it
Sensory details feel specifically remembered rather than generically invented

rubric.name: Narrative Momentum
rubric.description: Every beat — anecdote, detail, aside — should be load-bearing, pointing forward into what comes next and connecting outward to something beyond itself. Key arrivals and emotional moments feel earned because the reader has enough context about the journey and circumstances to invest. Personal backstory pulls double weight: it explains the narrator's situation AND illuminates the subject, place, or theme. The story should never feel like it starts at chapter two.

dimensions.label: Each anecdote, detail, or aside opens into something — setting up or connecting to what follows — rather than landing as a closing punchline
Key moments are preceded by enough journey or context that they feel earned rather than starting abruptly
Details and observations serve a discernible purpose in the larger piece — they are not just charming but directionless
The reader understands enough of the circumstances — how the writer got here, what they were feeling, what went before — to invest in what happens next
Personal backstory and anecdotes connect outward to illuminate the subject, setting, or theme — not just explain the narrator's circumstances

rubric.name: Warm Conversational Voice
rubric.description: The first-person narrator feels like a real person telling a story to a friend — intimate, approachable, never pretentious. The narrator is present as a person in the scene, not just as a skilled observer of it. There should be at least one moment where the narrator's own reaction or self-consciousness reveals something true about them in the experience.

dimensions.label: The narrator feels like a real person talking, not a writer composing prose
The tone treats the reader as a friend hearing a story, not an audience being performed to
At least one moment reveals the narrator as slightly out of their depth, self-conscious, or honestly reacting — making them feel present in the scene, not above it
Sentences vary as they would in natural, engaged storytelling — fragments, asides, and full sentences mixed
Word choice is vivid but accessible — no pretension or showing off

rubric.description: Sensory details should be precise and surprising enough that a reader stops and rereads — the 'read it to someone out loud' test. Comparisons should feel fresh and original. dimensions.label: Sensory descriptions use fresh, unexpected comparisons rather than familiar clichés

rubric.name: Narrative Momentum

rubric.name: Warm Conversational Voice
rubric.description: The first-person narrator feels like a real person inside the experience, not a polished observer watching it. The tone is intimate and approachable — like telling a story to a friend. The narrator must exist as a character in the scene: doing things, having physical reactions, being caught in honest moments of self-consciousness or vulnerability. A narrator who only watches calmly and clearly is a camera on a tripod, not a person.

The tone treats the reader as a friend hearing a story, not an audience being performed to

Word choice is vivid but accessible — no pretension or showing off

rubric.name: Emotional Restraint
rubric.description: Emotional significance should be shown through concrete moments and trusted to land on its own — never oversold, summarized, or announced. The essay should not decide what it means and tell the reader. Endings close with an image, sound, or quiet observation that keeps the reader inside the scene rather than pulling them out to look back at it from a distance.

dimensions.label: Emotional significance is conveyed through concrete moments rather than commentary that tells the reader what to feel
Key emotional beats are understated — they land without announcing their own importance
The piece closes with something the reader can see, hear, or feel — keeping them in the scene rather than pulling back to summarize or reflect from a distance
Emotional moments feel earned by what precedes them rather than reaching for unearned feeling
The closing feels discovered rather than constructed — it does not wrap up the emotional arc so completely that the essay appears to have decided what it means

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: sensory detail must be multi-sensory and physically grounding — a scene that only works visually will always feel flat to this user. The narrator must be a person IN the experience, not a skilled observer of it; when feedback says the piece feels like 'watching through glass' or 'a camera on a tripod,' the fix is always a small moment of honest action or self-recognition. This user's quality test is 'would I read this sentence to someone out loud' — if a line passes that test, it stays; if the ending could be replaced by a thesis statement with no loss, it fails.
